segments.text: Part 4C : Ligand-binding and receptors

Ligand binding
Ligand: A molecules that binds non-covalently to a protein
Binding is reversible
Can be a small molecule:
Oxygen, serotonin, epinephrine, glucose, ion
Can be a protein:
Protein-protein interactions
Other:
DNA, or RNA
Binding is often associated with a conformation change within the protein

Molecular transport
Protein gathers ligand from one part of the cell/body
Ligand is released at another part of the cell/body
Binding is reversible
This is an equilibrium process
P + L         PL

Thermodynamic of ligand binding
Single ligand to single binding site
Fractional saturation (r): fraction of the protein bound to Ligand (range: 0  1)
Graph exhibits a hyperbolic curve
r = [PL]/([PL]+[P] )
Kd = [PL]/([P] [L])
r (fractional saturation

Dissociation constant ( Kd )
Kd = [L] where ½ of the protein is bound, ½ unbound
Kd = 1/ Ka ( Ka : association constant)
High Kd = low affinity
Low Kd = high affinity
P + L         PL

Receptors
Membrane protein receptors:
Bind to a ligand on one side of the membrane
Binding triggers a biological signal
Hormones, neurotransmitters, many drugs

Receptor signaling
How does ligand binding on one side of the cell membrane trigger a biological response on the other side of the membrane?
Ligand-induced oligomerization
Individual receptor molecules come together in response to the ligand
Ligand-induced confomational changes
R eceptor goes from an “off” to an “on” conformation in response to the ligand

Types of receptors
Single pass receptor tyrosine kinases
Composed of three domains:
1. Extracellular domain : responsible for ligand binding
2. A single transmembrane domain
3. Intracellular domain : A Tyrosine Kinase : an enzyme that uses ATP to add phosphate group to a tyrosine on a target protien

Receptor Tyrosine Kinsases (RTKs)
Single pass receptor tyrosine kinases
Binding of the ligand outside the cell activates the tyrosine kinase activity
This results in phosphorylation of a target protein within the cytoplasm
The target protein is now activated
Usually there is a phosphorylation cascade!

Phosphorylation cascade
All mediated by a series of kinases
Enzymes known as phosphatases remove the phosphates to reverse the process

Epidermal Growth Factor (EGF) and its receptor: Ligand -Induced Receptor Oligomerization can
Figure 32.10 (a) EGF receptor dimerization and activation involves binding of hormone to the extracellular domains of two receptor molecules.
Extracellular domain
(Ligand binding)
Transmembrane
domain
Intracellular domain (Enzyme activity)

EGF Receptor is Activated by Ligand-Induced Dimerization
Prior to EGF binding, EGF receptors exist as inactive monomers in the plasma membrane
EGF binding induces a conformation change and dimerization
Dimerization of the extracellular domains brings the intracellular domains together, activating the tyrosine kinase activity
Inactive Tyrosine kinase
A ctive   Tyrosine kinase

The Insulin Receptor Mediates Several Signaling Pathways
Insulin, a small heterodimeric peptide, is the most potent anabolic hormone known
Insulin is produced by the pancreas in response to high blood glucose levels (after a meal)
Insulin binding to its receptor triggers multiple metabolic responses: Mainly glucose uptake by cells
The insulin receptor is an RTK that catalyzes the phosphorylation of tyrosine residues of several intracellular substrates

Ligand-Induced Receptor Reorganization
Figure 32.10 (b) The insulin receptor is a preexisting tetramer.  Two insulin-binding sites are created by association of the extracellular domains.
Insulin

The Insulin Receptor Mediates Several Signaling Pathways
Note: the insulin receptor is not made of one continuous polypeptide chain like EGFR
A dimer of dimers (α 2 β 2 )
Subunits are linked via disulfide bonds

The G-Protein-Coupled Receptors Are 7-TMS Integral Membrane Proteins
The G-protein-coupled receptors (GPCRs) have 7 transmembrane helices.
Binding of hormone to a GPCR induces a conformation change that activates a GTP-binding protein, also known as a G protein
Activated G proteins trigger a variety of cellular effects, including activation of adenylyl and guanylyl cyclases , activation of phospholipases, and activation of ion channels

The G-Protein-Coupled Receptors Are 7-TMS Integral Membrane Proteins
The G-protein-coupled receptors (GPCRs )
Extremely important in biology and medicine:
Taste and smell (olfactory) receptors
Many Neurotransmitter receptors
Autonomic nervous system:
Blood pressure, heart rate, digestion, etc.
Serotonin, dopamine, opioid, THC receptors
Growth and metastasis of tumors
~ 40% of drugs on the market target GPCRs

The G-Protein-Coupled Receptors Are 7-TMS Integral Membrane Proteins
Figure 32.8 (a) The 7-transmembrane segment arrangement of the β 2 -adrenergic receptor in the membrane .
Binding of ligand activates a bound “G-protein” resulting in signal propagation
G-protein

The G-Protein-Coupled Receptors Are 7-TMS Integral Membrane Proteins
Figure 32.8 (b) The structure of a β 2 -adrenergic receptor

GPCR Signals are Transduced by G Proteins
The signals of GPCRs are transduced by GTP-binding proteins (G proteins)
Two kinds: " heterotrimeric G proteins" and "small G proteins"
The heterotrimeric G proteins consist of α -, β - and γ subunits
α -subunit binds GDP or GTP and has an intrinsic GTPase activity: convert GTP to GDP

GPCR Signals are Transduced by G Proteins
Figure 32.21 A heterotrimeric G protein ( α – pink, β – yellow, γ – blue) docked with a β 2 -adrenergic receptor (green).
GPCR
G-protein

GPCR activation
Binding of hormone to receptor stimulates GTP-GDP exchange, causing G α to dissociate from G βγ and to associate with an effector protein
Effector proteins produce a Second Messenger
Effector proteins

GPCR activation
In the absence of ligand, GPCR is bond to G-protein
G α subunit is bound to GDP (inactive)

GPCR activation
When ligand binds, G-protein dissociates
G α subunit exchanges GDP for GTP (active)
Activates Effector proteins in one or more ways

GPCR activation
Two main effector proteins
Adenylate cyclase (AC): ATP  cAMP + PPi
Phospholipase C (PLC): breaks down phopsholipid
Both cases: A second messenger is produced

Adenylate cyclase produces cAMP (2 nd messenger)
Cyclic AMP is synthesized by membrane-bound adenylyl cyclase and degraded by soluble phosphodiesterase
cAMP can activate downstream effectors to propagate signal
Synthesis or Release of a Second Messenger is Followed Quickly by Degradation or Removal
Activation of some GPCRs is inhibitory: result in low cAMP

Two Stages of Amplification Occur in the G-Protein-Mediated Hormone Reponse
Figure 32.22 Adenylyl cyclase activity is modulated by the interplay of stimulatory ( G s ) and inhibitory ( G i ) proteins. Hormone binding to β 1 - and β 2 - receptors activates adenylyl cyclase , whereas hormone binding to α 2 -receptors leads to inhibition of adenylyl cyclase .

What is a second messenger?
The hormone is the "first messenger"
The second messenger - Ca 2+ , cAMP or other - is released when the hormone binds to its (extracellular) receptor
The second messenger then activates (or inhibits) processes in the cytosol or nucleus
Degradation and/or clearance of the second messenger is also (obviously) important

GPCR restoration
At some point, the G-protein will convert GTP to GDP
This will cause the G-protein to bind to the GPCR again (if there is no ligand)
“Inactive” form will be restored

Two Stages of Amplification Occur in the G-Protein-Mediated Hormone Reponse
First stage: a single hormone-receptor complex can activate many G proteins before the hormone dissociates from the receptor
Second stage: G α -activated adenylyl cyclase synthesizes many cAMP molecules
Thus binding of a hormone to a receptor stimulates a large increase in concentration of cAMP in the cell

Two Stages of Amplification Occur in the G-Protein-Mediated Hormone Reponse
The hormone receptor, G protein, and cyclase constitute a hormone signal transduction unit
G protein regulation may either be stimulatory or inhibitory – each receptor interacts both with stimulatory G s or inhibitory G i
G βγ also acts as a cell signal

IP3 as a Second Messenger: produced by phospholipid breakdown
A diverse array of second messengers is generated by breakdown of membrane phospholipids
Binding of some hormones to their receptors triggers activation of specific phospholipase C
Results in the breakdown of Inositol phospholipids to yield IP 3 and DAG ( diacylglycerol )
inositol
DAG
IP 3

IP3 as a second messenger
Inositol triphosphate
T he breakdown of Inositol phospholipids yields IP 3 and DAG
IP3: inositol-1,4,5-triphosphate
And DAG: diacylglycerol
IP3 acts as a second messenger
Activates downstream signaling

Activation of Phospholipase C is Mediated by G Proteins or by Tyrosine Kinases
Figure 32.28 Phospholipase C- β is activated specifically by G q (a GTP-binding protein) and also by Ca 2+

Types of G-proteins
Gs : stimulatory, leads to activation of AC, increase in cAMP levels
Gi : inhibitory, leads to deactivation of AC, decrease in cAMP
Gq : activates phospholipase C, release IP3
S econd messenger molecules: cAMP , IP3
